Slow-cooked lamb with pearl onions, wine, and spices creating a rich, tender stew served with crusty bread to soak up the sauce.
Simply boiled wild greens served with crumbly feta cheese and olive oil, showcasing Crete's emphasis on fresh, locally-foraged ingredients.
A semi-hard Cretan cheese with a subtle nutty flavor, aged from local milk and often served fried as saganaki at tavernas.
Rooster or beef braised in tomato sauce and wine, a Venetian-influenced dish particular to Rethymno that reflects Crete's cultural heritage.
Honey-drizzled fried dough puffs often sprinkled with walnuts or cinnamon, a beloved sweet treat found at street vendors and bakeries.

culture.detail: Respect the afternoon siesta (roughly 2-5 PM) when many shops close and locals rest; plan your activities accordingly rather than expect full service.
Cretans are known for genuine warmth and generosity; accepting offered coffee, drinks, or small gifts is customary and appreciated as a sign of respect.
Dining is a social event—meals often last hours, waiters won't rush you, and it's common for the taverna owner to offer complimentary dessert or raki.
If visiting during Easter, experience intense local traditions including processions, fireworks, and the preparation of lamb roasts that dominate village life.

restaurants.name: Taverna Negroponte
restaurants.description: Family-run taverna serving authentic Cretan dishes in a charming old town setting with harbor views.

restaurants.name: Rigas Taverna
restaurants.description: Waterfront taverna specializing in fresh fish and seafood with exceptional service and sunset views.

restaurants.name: Portes
restaurants.description: Elegant restaurant in a restored Venetian building offering modern takes on traditional Cretan cuisine.
restaurants.mustOrder: Feta saganaki with honey and sesame

restaurants.name: Gorgona
restaurants.description: Traditional seafood taverna with a relaxed atmosphere, fresh daily catches, and reasonable prices.

restaurants.name: Loukoulos
restaurants.description: Upscale dining experience with creative Mediterranean cuisine and an impressive wine selection.

restaurants.name: Iordanis
restaurants.description: No-frills family eatery serving honest, delicious Cretan home cooking at very affordable prices.

restaurants.name: Avli Restaurant
restaurants.description: Romantic garden restaurant in a traditional Cretan house offering refined local specialties and wine pairings.

restaurants.name: To Kanoni
restaurants.description: Welcoming meze bar with an extensive selection of small plates and local wines in a cozy setting.

restaurants.name: Peskesi
restaurants.description: Award-winning restaurant celebrating traditional Cretan ingredients with contemporary presentation and technique.

restaurants.name: Ta Chania
restaurants.description: Casual neighborhood taverna known for generous portions of authentic Cretan fare and warm hospitality.

restaurants.name: Sitia Meze & Wine
restaurants.description: Contemporary meze restaurant showcasing local ingredients and Cretan wines in Crete's eastern gem.

restaurants.name: Levantine
restaurants.description: Modern restaurant combining Levantine and Cretan influences with fresh seafood and creative preparations.
restaurants.mustOrder: Sea bass with Cretan olive oil and herbs

restaurants.name: Tamam
restaurants.description: Sophisticated tapas bar in a restored Ottoman building serving creative small plates and craft cocktails.

restaurants.name: O Glentis
restaurants.description: Traditional taverna with a lively atmosphere, live music some nights, and excellent local dishes.

regions.name: Attica
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from April to May or September to October for pleasant weather and fewer crowds around the ancient sites.

regions.name: Central Greece
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Best visited in spring or autumn to enjoy the Meteora monasteries and mountain scenery with moderate temperatures.

regions.name: Thessaly
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from April to May or September to October for ideal weather to explore Mount Pelion and the Meteora.

regions.name: Epirus
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Best visited in late spring through early autumn to experience the Vikos Gorge and mountain villages in pleasant weather.

regions.name: Western Greece
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from May to October for warm weather perfect for beach exploration and the charming towns of the Peloponnese coast.

regions.name: Peloponnese
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Best visited in spring or autumn for comfortable exploration of ancient ruins and coastal towns.

regions.name: Ionian Islands
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from May to September for warm, sunny weather ideal for island hopping and water activities.

regions.name: Central Macedonia
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Best visited from April to May or September to October for exploring the city and nearby Mount Olympus with pleasant temperatures.

regions.name: Eastern Macedonia and Thrace
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from May to September to explore the mountainous terrain and Black Sea coast with comfortable weather.

regions.name: Sporades
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Best visited from May to September for perfect beach weather and island exploration in the Aegean Sea.

regions.name: Northern Aegean Islands
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from May to September for warm weather and excellent conditions for exploring the larger islands and their wineries.

regions.name: Southern Aegean
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Best visited from April to May or September to October for warm, sunny weather without extreme summer heat.

regions.name: Crete
regions.bestTimeToVisit: Visit from April to May or September to October for enjoyable beach weather and exploration of Minoan ruins.

top10.name: Acropolis, Athens
top10.description: The ancient hilltop citadel crowned by the Parthenon, dating to the 5th century BCE, stands as one of the world's most iconic symbols of Classical Greek civilization. Millions visit annually to witness the architectural masterpiece and explore the museums housing ancient Greek artifacts.

top10.name: Athens
top10.description: Greece's vibrant capital and largest city, home to the iconic Acropolis, Parthenon, and countless ancient monuments alongside modern museums and bustling neighborhoods. The birthplace of Western democracy offers world-class antiquities, excellent food, and the energy of a contemporary Mediterranean metropolis.

top10.name: Santorini
top10.description: This volcanic Cycladic island is famous for its white-washed buildings with blue domes perched on dramatic cliffs overlooking the caldera and Aegean Sea. Visitors flock here for the spectacular sunsets, pristine beaches, and romantic atmosphere that have made it one of Europe's most photographed destinations.

top10.name: Mykonos
top10.description: This glamorous Cycladic island is renowned for its vibrant nightlife, cosmopolitan atmosphere, and beautiful beaches lined with upscale clubs and beach bars. Tourists visit for the energetic party scene, trendy restaurants, and the picturesque Cycladic charm of its chora town.

top10.name: Delphi
top10.description: Ancient sanctuary of Apollo and one of Greece's most sacred sites, perched dramatically on Mount Parnassus overlooking the valley below. The archaeological site features the iconic Temple of Apollo, the ancient theatre, and the museum housing the famous Charioteer statue, making it essential for understanding Classical Greek civilization.

top10.name: Crete
top10.description: Greece's largest island offers a unique blend of Minoan archaeological sites, stunning beaches, traditional villages, and diverse landscapes from beaches to mountains. Tourists are drawn to its rich history, excellent food, warm hospitality, and variety of activities including water sports and cultural exploration.

top10.name: Thessaloniki
top10.description: Greece's second-largest city and capital of Central Macedonia, famous for its Byzantine churches, Ottoman architecture, and vibrant cultural scene. Essential for its museums, waterfront promenade, and as a gateway to the region's attractions.

top10.name: Rhodes
top10.description: This southeastern Dodecanese island is famous for its pristine beaches, medieval Old Town with the Palace of Grand Masters, and pleasant climate year-round. Tourists flock here for water sports, historical exploration, nightlife, and easy access to nearby islands and Turkey.

top10.name: Meteora
top10.description: Otherworldly landscape of towering rock pillars topped with ancient monasteries, creating one of the most visually stunning spiritual sites in Europe. Visitors can explore the monasteries perched atop these natural formations and hike among the dramatic geology that has captivated pilgrims and travelers for centuries.

top10.name: Olympia
top10.description: The birthplace of the Olympic Games, this archaeological site in the Peloponnese contains the ruins of temples, stadiums, and training grounds dating back millennia. History enthusiasts and sports fans visit to walk in the footsteps of ancient athletes and explore the museum housing the famous Statue of Zeus.

guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 40 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: New Year's Day
festivals.description: Greece's primary national holiday marking the start of the year with family gatherings, traditional meals, and the custom of cutting the New Year's cake.

festivals.name: Epiphany (Theophania)
festivals.description: A Christian celebration featuring the blessing of the waters ceremony where crosses are tossed into harbors and seas across Greece for young divers to retrieve.

festivals.name: Carnival Season (Apokries)
festivals.description: A week-long celebration before Lent featuring colorful parades, masquerades, and merrymaking, with the famous Patras Carnival being Greece's largest and most vibrant.

festivals.name: Othomano Carnival
festivals.description: Thessaloniki's version of carnival featuring elaborate floats, satirical performances, and street celebrations reflecting the city's unique cultural identity.

festivals.name: Skyros Carnival
festivals.description: An ancient carnival tradition featuring the Geri dancers in shaggy costumes and bell-wearing characters that dates back to pagan fertility rites.

festivals.name: Clean Monday (Kathara Deftera)
festivals.description: The start of Lent marked by flying kites and eating traditional fasting foods like lagana and fava, celebrated throughout Greece with family gatherings.

festivals.name: Independence Day
festivals.description: Greece's national holiday commemorating the start of the Greek War of Independence in 1821, celebrated with military parades, flag-raising ceremonies, and patriotic events.

festivals.name: Annunciation of Mary
festivals.description: A religious holiday coinciding with Independence Day, celebrating the Angel Gabriel's announcement to Mary in Orthodox tradition.

festivals.name: Flower Festival of Rhodes
festivals.description: A spring celebration in Rhodes featuring flower displays, garden exhibits, and botanical showcases celebrating the island's natural beauty.

festivals.name: Easter (Pascha)
festivals.description: Orthodox Easter is the most important religious holiday in Greece featuring midnight resurrection services, the lighting of candles, and celebratory feasts with lamb and traditional foods.

festivals.name: Spring Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates the arrival of spring in Volos and surrounding areas with flower exhibitions, cultural performances, and agricultural displays.

festivals.name: Feast of St. George
festivals.description: A significant religious observance featuring traditional processions and celebrations honoring Greece's patron saint with cultural events and feasting.

festivals.name: Rethymno Carnival
festivals.description: Crete's vibrant pre-Lenten celebration featuring parades with elaborate floats, masked balls, and Venetian-influenced festivities reflecting the island's history.

festivals.name: Labour Day
festivals.description: Greece's national holiday celebrating workers' rights, marked by parades and gatherings in Athens and other cities throughout the country.

festivals.name: Flower Festival of Corfu
festivals.description: A spring celebration in Corfu featuring flower displays and botanical exhibitions showcasing the island's lush Mediterranean flora.

festivals.name: Ascension of Christ
festivals.description: A significant Orthodox Christian holiday celebrated 40 days after Easter commemorating Christ's ascension to heaven.

festivals.name: Pentecost (Whit Monday)
festivals.description: The Monday after Pentecost is a national holiday in Greece featuring traditional customs and family celebrations.

festivals.name: Athens Festival
festivals.description: A major cultural festival featuring theater, music, and dance performances held in historic venues including the Odeon of Herodes Atticus.

festivals.name: Epidaurus Festival
festivals.description: An ancient theater festival featuring classical Greek plays and modern performances held in the remarkably preserved ancient theater of Epidaurus.

festivals.name: Rethymno Wine Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of Cretan wine culture featuring tastings, traditional music, dancing, and local cuisine in a festive town square setting.

festivals.name: Feast of the Holy Apostles
festivals.description: A nationwide religious holiday celebrating the apostles Peter and Paul with church services and traditional observances across Greece.

festivals.name: Chios Mastic Festival
festivals.description: An annual celebration of mastic production featuring food stalls, traditional crafts, music, and the famous mastic liqueur unique to Chios.

festivals.name: Assumption of Mary
festivals.description: One of Greece's most important religious holidays celebrated throughout the country with church services, processions, and family gatherings.

festivals.name: Rockwertigo Festival
festivals.description: A summer rock music festival in Rethymno featuring international and Greek rock bands performing on outdoor stages.

festivals.name: Hellenic Air Force Week
festivals.description: An annual celebration featuring the famous Hellenic Air Force acrobatic team demonstrations over Athens.

festivals.name: Kalamata International Dance Festival
festivals.description: A prestigious festival featuring contemporary and traditional dance performances from Greece and around the world.

festivals.name: Thessaloniki International Film Festival
festivals.description: One of Greece's most important film festivals showcasing international cinema and promoting Thessaloniki as a cultural hub.

festivals.name: Oenological Festival of Alexandroupoli
festivals.description: A celebration of Thracian wines featuring tastings, regional cuisine, and cultural performances in this northeastern Greek port city.

festivals.name: Gazi Festival
festivals.description: An annual cultural festival in Athens' regenerated Gazi district featuring theater, music, dance, and street art performances.

festivals.name: Oktoberfest Athens
festivals.description: Greece's version of the famous beer festival featuring Bavarian-style entertainment, food, and beer celebrations in Athens.

festivals.name: Ochi Day
festivals.description: Greece's national holiday commemorating the country's 'no' (ochi) to the Italian ultimatum in 1940, celebrated with military parades and patriotic events.

festivals.name: Documenta Film Festival
festivals.description: Greece's leading documentary film festival held in Thessaloniki showcasing innovative documentaries from around the world.

festivals.name: Feast of Saint Demetrius
festivals.description: A significant religious celebration in Thessaloniki honoring the city's patron saint with processions and religious services.

festivals.name: Raki Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of Cretan raki spirit featuring traditional distillation demonstrations, tastings, and Cretan music and dancing.

festivals.name: Lights Festival of Athens
festivals.description: An illumination festival transforming Athens' neighborhoods with artistic light installations and cultural events.

festivals.name: Feast of the Archangels Michael and Gabriel
festivals.description: A significant Orthodox religious holiday celebrated across Greece with church services and traditional observances.

festivals.name: Christmas
festivals.description: Greece's Christian holiday celebrated with family gatherings, traditional foods, church services, and festive decorations throughout the country.

festivals.name: Feast of the Nativity
festivals.description: The Orthodox celebration of Christ's birth observed as a major religious holiday across Greece.

festivals.name: Synaxis of the Mother of God
festivals.description: An Orthodox religious holiday celebrated the day after Christmas honoring the Mother of God with church services and traditional observances.

festivals.name: New Year's Eve Celebrations
festivals.description: Greece's year-end celebration featuring festive gatherings, traditional music, dancing, and the custom of the New Year's cake fortune.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of Greece
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 13 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Neolithic Settlement
items.summary: Early farming communities establish permanent villages across Greece and the Aegean islands.
items.text: Greece's first inhabitants arrived during the Paleolithic era, but it was the Neolithic period that saw the birth of civilization. Farmers from Anatolia migrated southward, bringing agriculture and domesticated animals, transforming hunter-gatherer societies into settled communities. Villages like those in Thessaly developed pottery, tools, and social structures that would define Mediterranean life for millennia. The cultivation of wheat, barley, and olives, combined with sheep and goat herding, created surplus wealth that enabled trade and specialization. By 3000 BC, these Neolithic settlements had laid the groundwork for the great Bronze Age civilizations to follow.

items.label: Early Bronze Age & the Minoans
items.summary: The Minoan civilization on Crete becomes the Mediterranean's first advanced society, mastering trade and art.
items.text: The Bronze Age brought metallurgy and trade networks that transformed Greece into a cosmopolitan region. On Crete, the Minoan civilization emerged as Europe's first complex society, building palatial centers like Knossos with sophisticated plumbing, elaborate frescoes, and a written script still partly undeciphered. Minoans dominated Mediterranean commerce, exporting wine, oil, and pottery to Egypt, Syria, and beyond, while importing luxury goods that reflected their wealth and taste. Their influence spread throughout the Aegean islands and mainland Greece, shaping art, religion, and trade practices. Though their civilization mysteriously collapsed around 1100 BC—possibly due to volcanic eruption, invasion, or climate change—their cultural legacy profoundly influenced the classical Greeks.

items.label: Mycenaean Civilization
items.summary: Bronze Age warriors from mainland Greece build a powerful military civilization, creating the foundation for classical Greek culture.
items.text: As Minoan power waned, the Mycenaeans rose on mainland Greece, adapting Minoan innovations while developing their own militaristic society. These warrior kings built fortified palaces at Mycenae, Tiryns, and Pylos, ruled through feudal hierarchies, and recorded their language in Linear B script, which was an early form of Greek. The Mycenaeans were master shipbuilders and traders who competed with and eventually absorbed Minoan territories, spreading their influence across the eastern Mediterranean. Archaeological evidence suggests they may have fought the Trojan War immortalized in Homer's epics, though details remain disputed. By 1100 BC, however, their palatial civilization collapsed—perhaps from invasion, internal conflict, or climate crisis—plunging Greece into a centuries-long Dark Age.

items.label: The Greek Dark Age
items.summary: Greece retreats into isolated, small-scale communities, losing literacy and urban complexity for four centuries.
items.text: The collapse of Mycenaean civilization triggered a cultural and economic collapse so severe that later Greeks called it the Dark Age. Writing disappeared, populations declined, and Greeks regressed to subsistence farming and small tribal settlements scattered across the mainland and islands. Ironically, this period of apparent decline witnessed crucial cultural developments: the transition from bronze to iron tools, the evolution of the Greek language, and the emergence of the polis, the city-state that would become the organizing principle of classical Greece. During this time, Homer composed or refined the Iliad and Odyssey, stories that would define Greek identity and values. By 800 BC, trade routes reopened, populations grew, and the alphabet arrived from Phoenician traders, setting the stage for the birth of classical civilization.

items.label: The Archaic Period & Rise of City-States
items.summary: Greece develops the polis system, colonizes the Mediterranean, and creates the foundations of democracy and classical culture.
items.text: The Archaic period saw explosive growth, cultural renaissance, and political innovation as independent city-states became the fundamental unit of Greek life. Athens and Sparta emerged as rivals: Athens developed a merchant economy and eventually pioneered democracy, while Sparta built a rigid militaristic society. Greeks colonized extensively, establishing settlements from Spain to the Black Sea, spreading their culture and creating wealthy trading networks. This era saw the birth of the Olympic Games in 776 BC, the development of Greek philosophy through thinkers like Thales, and a revolution in art and pottery that reflected growing prosperity. By 480 BC, these city-states would unite—however fractiously—against the Persian Empire, but the competitive, independent spirit of the polis remained the defining feature of Greek civilization.

items.label: Classical Period & Persian Wars
items.summary: Greek city-states defeat Persia and enter a golden age of philosophy, democracy, and artistic achievement that defines Western civilization.
items.text: When the vast Persian Empire invaded Greece, the city-states united reluctantly but heroically, defeating invaders at Marathon, Salamis, and Plataea in a series of stunning victories that made them the underdogs who vanquished an oriental colossus. Athens emerged victorious and dominant, using its new prestige to build a maritime empire and foster an unprecedented flowering of culture: Socrates questioned conventional wisdom, Plato founded his Academy, Aristotle developed logic and science, while sculptors and architects created the Parthenon and other monuments that set aesthetic standards for millennia. Under Pericles, Athens pioneered direct democracy where free male citizens voted on major decisions, and the culture of debate and reason became central to Greek identity. Philosophy, drama, history-writing, and science all achieved heights that subsequent civilizations would struggle to match, making this brief century perhaps the most influential in Western history. Yet constant warfare between Athens and Sparta drained resources and unity, eventually leading to the ruinous Peloponnesian War.

items.label: Hellenistic Age
items.summary: Alexander the Great conquers the known world, spreading Greek culture across three continents before his empire fragments.
items.text: When Alexander the Great inherited the Macedonian throne, he launched the most audacious military campaign in ancient history, crushing Persian forces and conquering territories from Egypt to India in just thirteen years. Though he died at thirty-two, his conquests spread Greek language, culture, and ideas across an unprecedented geographic area, creating a cosmopolitan Hellenistic world where Greek became the lingua franca of commerce and learning. His generals divided his empire into competing kingdoms—Egypt under the Ptolemies, Syria under the Seleucids, Macedonia under the Antigonadids—each a cultural powerhouse supporting philosophers, mathematicians, poets, and scientists. The Hellenistic period saw remarkable scientific advances, from Euclid's geometry to Archimedes' physics, and new philosophical schools like Stoicism and Epicureanism that offered different paths to human flourishing. However, endless dynastic wars weakened these Greek-ruled territories, leaving them vulnerable to a new superpower rising in the west: Rome.

items.label: Roman Greece
items.summary: Rome conquers Greece and incorporates it into its empire, though Greek culture profoundly influences Roman civilization.
items.text: Roman legions defeated the last independent Greek kingdoms at the Battle of Corinth in 146 BC, making Greece a Roman province, yet the conquerors became cultural captives of the conquered. Romans adopted Greek gods (renaming them), studied Greek philosophy, imitated Greek art, and educated their children in Greek language and literature, making it clear that military defeat did not mean cultural defeat. Greece became a popular destination for wealthy Romans seeking education and cultural refinement, while Greek scholars and artists found lucrative patronage in Rome. The Roman Peace allowed trade and travel to flourish, and many Greek cities prospered despite their loss of political independence. When the Roman Empire split in 330 AD, Greece fell under the Byzantine Eastern Roman Empire, which would maintain Greco-Roman civilization for another thousand years.

items.label: Byzantine Greece
items.summary: Greece becomes part of the Byzantine Empire, preserving Greek language, Orthodox Christianity, and classical learning through medieval centuries.
items.text: When Constantine founded Constantinople and made it capital of the eastern Roman Empire, Greece became part of a Byzantine realm that saw itself as the continuation of Rome but increasingly spoke and thought in Greek. The Byzantine Empire faced constant threats from Goths, Persians, Arabs, and eventually Turks, yet maintained the Greek language, Greek Orthodox Christianity, and a connection to classical learning that western Europe largely lost during the medieval period. Byzantine scholars preserved Greek texts that might otherwise have vanished, and monasteries on Mount Athos became centers of Orthodox spirituality and learning. Though Crusaders sacked Constantinople in 1204 and ruled parts of Greece for two centuries, Byzantine Greek culture reasserted itself and maintained continuity with the classical past. When the Ottomans finally conquered Constantinople in 1453, ending the Byzantine Empire, Greece fell under Turkish rule for the next four centuries, yet Greeks maintained their language, Orthodox faith, and cultural identity.

items.label: Ottoman Greece
items.summary: Ottoman Turks rule Greece for four centuries, though Greeks preserve their language, religion, and identity through community and church.
items.text: The Ottoman conquest initially brought chaos and displacement, but the Ottoman system eventually allowed Greek Christians to maintain their Orthodox Church, language, and customs in exchange for taxes and political subordination. Greeks became merchants, sailors, administrators, and intellectuals within the Ottoman system, some achieving considerable wealth and influence, while peasants worked the land under Ottoman or Greek Christian landlords. The Church became the primary institution preserving Greek identity and education, with monasteries and parish priests maintaining Greek language and Orthodox traditions even as Turkish became the language of administration. By the eighteenth century, Greek merchants had grown wealthy through Mediterranean trade, created schools where Greek language and classics were taught, and developed a sense of national identity rooted in Orthodox Christianity, the Greek language, and memories of Byzantine and ancient glory. This growing sense of Greekness, combined with Ottoman decline and European revolutionary ideals, created the conditions for uprising.

items.label: Greek War of Independence
items.summary: Greeks revolt against Ottoman rule and, after a brutal eight-year war, establish an independent nation-state.
items.text: When Greeks rose in rebellion in 1821, they faced an Ottoman Empire that viewed them as rebellious subjects rather than a people with national aspirations, leading to brutal reprisals that killed hundreds of thousands and displaced many more. The Greek cause attracted support from European Philhellenes who romanticized ancient Greece, and European powers—Britain, France, Russia—intervened militarily and diplomatically, partly from sympathy and partly from geopolitical interest in Ottoman decline. After eight years of devastating guerrilla warfare, massacres, and naval battles, Greek forces under leaders like Ioannis Kapodistrias secured independence in 1829 through the Treaty of Adrianople. However, the new Greek state was far smaller than the vast areas where Greeks lived under Ottoman rule—it included only the mainland and some islands, leaving millions of Greeks under Ottoman or other foreign rule. A young European prince, Otto of Bavaria, was chosen as the first king of independent Greece, bringing foreign institutions and foreign rule to a nation that had just freed itself.

items.label: Modern Greece & National Struggles
items.summary: Greece builds a nation-state while struggling to unite all Greek territories, recover from wars, and navigate European politics.
items.text: The nineteenth century saw Greece gradually expand territory through wars and diplomacy, gaining Thessaly and other regions while Crete and the islands remained under Ottoman or other foreign rule until the twentieth century. A devastating defeat by Turkey in 1922 caused massive population exchanges, bringing hundreds of thousands of Greek refugees from Anatolia but creating enormous social strain and trauma. Greece endured military dictatorships, coups, civil conflict between monarchists and republicans, and devastating participation in both world wars. The Second World War brought Nazi occupation, starvation, and Holocaust horrors to Greek Jews, while the country itself was liberated only to descend into a brutal civil war between communists and royalists. By 1945, Greece emerged victorious but exhausted, its territory largely unified but its economy shattered, its people traumatized, and its political future uncertain.

items.label: Modern Democratic Greece
items.summary: Greece rebuilds as a Western democracy, joins NATO and the EU, and navigates Mediterranean politics while preserving its cultural heritage.
items.text: With American Marshall Plan aid and integration into the Western bloc during the Cold War, Greece gradually rebuilt its economy and established a stable if sometimes contentious democracy. The 1967 military junta shocked the nation but ultimately led to a democratic restoration and the 1975 Constitution that remains the foundation of Greek governance. Greece joined NATO in 1952, the European Union in 1981, and the Eurozone in 2002, binding itself to Western institutions and the European project. The country faced ongoing tensions with Turkey over Cyprus, the Aegean, and competing claims, as well as internal challenges of bureaucratic reform and economic development, though it achieved remarkable progress in education and living standards. In recent decades, Greece has become a major tourist destination where visitors marvel at ancient ruins, Byzantine monasteries, and Mediterranean islands, connecting modern Greeks to their extraordinary historical legacy while the country itself grapples with economic crises, demographic challenges, and its role in a changing Europe and world.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in Greece — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Greek coffee is thick and strong
items.text: Order "elliniko" (Greek coffee) for the traditional preparation—finely ground coffee brewed in a small copper pot with water and sugar, served in a tiny cup with grounds settling at the bottom. Ask for "sketo" (no sugar), "metrio" (medium), or "gliko" (very sweet) when ordering.

items.label: Kafeneia are social spaces, not quick stops
items.text: Greeks linger for hours over a single coffee or ouzo, reading newspapers and playing backgammon. Sitting down means you're welcome to stay as long as you like; the waiter won't rush you or bring the bill uninvited.

items.label: Frappe is the iced coffee of choice
items.text: This ubiquitous iced drink (instant coffee whipped with water until foamy, served with cold milk and ice) is perfect for hot afternoons and costs just €2-3 everywhere.

items.label: Avoid tourist-trap cafés near major sites
items.text: A coffee in Plaka or near the Acropolis can cost double what it does one street back. Greeks themselves drink at humble neighbourhood kafeneia where quality is better and prices honest.

items.label: Menus in Greek regions rarely have English translations
items.text: In rural areas and smaller islands, Greek-only menus are the norm—ask your waiter to explain dishes or point to what other tables are eating. This is often a sign of authenticity.

items.label: Look for dishes listed without prices
items.text: These are seasonal and priced by weight; ask the waiter before ordering. Grilled fish, lamb, and vegetables are typically priced this way and can be excellent value or surprisingly expensive depending on the day.

items.label: Starters (mezze) are meant for sharing
items.text: Greeks order multiple small plates to share rather than individual mains—this is the traditional way to eat and often gives better value and variety than a single large plate.

items.label: Ask what the house special is
items.text: Waiters will steer you toward what's freshest and best today, not what's most profitable. Trusting their recommendation often leads to better food than studying the menu yourself.

items.label: Moussaka
items.text: Layers of eggplant, spiced meat sauce, and creamy béchamel, baked until golden. It's Greece's most famous export and worth trying at a proper taverna, though tourist versions are often mediocre.

items.label: Souvlaki and Keftedes
items.text: Grilled meat skewers (souvlaki) and meatballs (keftedes) served with pita, tzatziki, and onions—the ultimate street food and entirely affordable even in tourist areas.

items.label: Horta with sea salt
items.text: Simply boiled wild greens (whatever is seasonal) served with lemon and olive oil. Unglamorous but quintessentially Greek, and a staple side dish you'll find everywhere.

items.label: Saganaki (fried cheese)
items.text: Thick slices of cheese (usually graviera or kasseri) pan-fried until golden with a melted interior—often dramatically set alight at the table with a theatrical "opa!"

items.label: Psarosoupa (fish soup) and Htipotο (octopus)
items.text: Coastal regions serve exceptional seafood; grilled octopus drizzled with lemon is simple, perfect, and a marker of quality. Fish soup varies by region and season.

items.label: Feta with honey and walnuts
items.text: Often served as an unexpected dessert or cheese course—creamy feta, warm honey, and crunchy walnuts create an addictive sweet-savoury contrast.

items.label: Taverna vs. Estiatorio: know the difference
items.text: A taverna is casual, family-run, with simple grilled food and wine from the barrel; an estiatorio is more formal with table service, printed menus, and wine lists. Both can be excellent; choose based on mood.

items.label: Seek out places with Greek families, not tourists
items.text: If a taverna is full of locals eating Sunday lunch, the food is almost certainly good and priced fairly. Tourist-focused restaurants with photo menus and staff touting specials should be avoided.

items.label: Waterfront restaurants in touristy ports are overpriced
items.text: Walk one or two streets back from the harbour and you'll find the same food at half the price and three times the authenticity. Locals know better than to eat where the boats tie up.

items.label: Lunch (1–3 pm) is the main meal, dinner is light
items.text: Restaurants are fullest at lunch; arriving then gives you access to the day's best dishes and a more genuine Greek dining experience. Dinner is often quieter and later (after 9 pm).

items.label: Small neighbourhood psarotaverna (fish taverna) are worth seeking out
items.text: These humble spots, often with plastic chairs and checked tablecloths, serve the freshest seafood at fair prices. Ask locals or your hotel keeper for recommendations rather than relying on reviews.

items.label: Ouzo is the national spirit, not a casual shot
items.text: Sip ouzo slowly (it's 40% alcohol), diluted with water until cloudy, over ice—never shoot it. It's a social drink meant to accompany food and conversation, usually ordered at the end of a meal.

items.label: Greek wine is underrated and excellent value
items.text: Ask for local wines by region: Santorini (crisp whites), Nemea (bold reds), or Retsina (traditional pine-resin white). House wine (krasi hima) at tavernas is usually €3–5 per glass and often very good.

items.label: Retsina tastes like pine trees—embrace or avoid it
items.text: This ancient white wine, flavoured with pine resin, is an acquired taste but quintessentially Greek. Try it once; Greeks either love it or avoid it entirely, and there's no middle ground.

items.label: Mythos and Alpha are the common beers
items.text: Both are clean lagers, perfectly adequate and cheap. Local craft beers are emerging in Athens but rare outside the capital.

items.label: Never drink alone—alcohol comes with food and company
items.text: Greeks rarely order a drink without ordering food, and public drinking (outside a bar or restaurant) is uncommon and frowned upon. Alcohol is a social ritual, not a solitary indulgence.

items.label: Bread is served free and expected; leaving it is wasteful
items.text: Bread arrives unbidden and uncharged. Finish what you can or ask the waiter to remove the basket—leaving bread uneaten on the table is considered disrespectful to the food.

items.label: Say 'Kalí órexi' before eating; it means 'bon appétit'
items.text: Greeks say this to neighbouring tables and it's polite to return the greeting. It's a small gesture that signals respect for the meal and the company.

items.label: Tipping: round up or leave 5–10%
items.text: Tipping is not mandatory but rounding up the bill or leaving 5–10% for good service is customary. Card payments often don't have a tip line; ask the waiter if you wish to add cash.

items.label: The bill won't arrive unless you ask
items.text: Ask for 'to logariasmo' (the bill) when ready; waiting tables are not a signal the restaurant wants you to leave. Greeks linger for hours after eating; rushing diners out is considered rude.

items.label: Refuse food or drink to show respect, then accept
items.text: If offered something (especially by an older person or at someone's home), a polite initial refusal is traditional before accepting. It shows you're not greedy or presumptuous.

items.label: Compliment the cook if the meal was exceptional
items.text: Greeks take genuine pride in food. A simple 'bravo' or 'poli nosto' (very tasty) to the owner or waiter will be remembered and appreciated—and might result in a free dessert or digestif.

items.label: Spanakopita and Tyropita
items.text: Crispy phyllo pastries filled with spinach-feta or cheese—perfect for a quick breakfast or snack from a bakery (psomadia). They're warm, cheap (€2–3), and more substantial than they look.

items.label: Greek breakfast is simple: coffee and pastry or toast
items.text: Don't expect a big cooked breakfast; Greeks typically have coffee and a pastry (often baklava or loukoumades—fried dough balls in honey) at a café, then a large lunch.

items.label: Loukoumades
items.text: Fried dough puffs drizzled with honey and sprinkled with walnuts or cinnamon—irresistible, indulgent, and usually found at street stalls or bakeries in the morning.

items.label: Bakeries (psomadia) are your friend for affordable eats
items.text: Every neighbourhood has at least one; buy fresh bread, pastries, and pre-made savoury pies for a quarter of what a café would charge. Quality varies, but finding a good one is worth it.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around Greece.

items.label: TrainOSE network
items.text: Greek Railways (TrainOSE) operates intercity services connecting major cities like Athens, Thessaloniki, and Patras. Book tickets via www.trainose.gr or at stations; advance booking offers discounts of 20-50% for most routes.

items.label: Athens-Thessaloniki route
items.text: The main north-south corridor has multiple daily departures and takes 4.5-5 hours depending on the train type. IC (InterCity) trains are faster but cost more than regional services.
Strikes by railway workers occur occasionally and can disrupt schedules with little notice; check the TrainOSE website or local news before traveling, especially during summer months.

items.label: Regional coverage limits
items.text: Train service is limited outside major routes; ferries and buses are more practical for island access and smaller towns.

items.label: Athens metro system
items.text: Athens has a modern, clean metro (Lines 1, 2, 3) covering the city and suburbs; single tickets cost €1.40 and are valid for 90 minutes across metro, trams, and buses. The metro runs 5:30 AM to midnight daily.

items.label: Multi-day passes
items.text: Buy a 3-day tourist pass (€22) or 7-day pass (€30) for unlimited travel on all public transport in Athens and Piraeus; these are the best value for visitors.

items.label: Bus networks
items.text: Most Greek cities have bus systems operated by local companies; Athens uses OASA. Buses are affordable but can be crowded during peak hours; validate your ticket using machines on board.
Pickpocketing is common on crowded buses and metro trains, especially on Line 3 to the airport and in tourist areas; keep bags zipped and valuables secure.

items.label: Thessaloniki transport
items.text: Greece's second city has metro, buses, and trams; a single ticket costs €0.90 and day passes are €3, making it cheaper than Athens.

items.label: Metered taxis
items.text: Licensed yellow taxis in Athens and Thessaloniki use meters; rates start at €3.20 (night surcharge +€1) and charge approximately €0.65 per km. Always ask the driver to use the meter or agree on a price beforehand.
Unlicensed taxis and ride-share hustlers operate at airports and ferry terminals offering inflated rates; use official taxi stands or ride-apps instead.

items.label: Beat (ride-hailing app)
items.text: Beat is Greece's main ride-sharing app, similar to Uber; fares are transparent, cheaper than traditional taxis, and available in Athens, Thessaloniki, and other major cities. Payment via credit card is required.

items.label: Airport transfers
items.text: From Athens Airport, expect €35-40 by metered taxi or €25-30 via Beat; the metro also connects to the airport for €10 (included in multi-day passes).
Taxi drivers rarely speak English outside major cities; use a map app or Beat to show your destination clearly and avoid confusion.

items.label: License & documents
items.text: International Driving Permit (IDP) plus your home country license are required; car rental also requires a credit card and minimum age (usually 21, sometimes 25 for luxury vehicles).

Greek driving is aggressive; expect speeding, tailgating, and frequent horn use. Locals ignore some traffic laws, but tourists are heavily fined for violations—always wear seatbelts and obey speed limits.

items.label: Mountain & island roads
items.text: Roads outside major cities are narrow, winding, and poorly lit at night; drive slowly, especially in summer when tourists crowd narrow village streets. Many island roads have gravel sections and steep drop-offs.

items.label: Speed limits & cameras
items.text: Speed limits are 50 km/h in towns, 90 km/h on rural roads, and 130 km/h on highways; speeding fines start at €100 and are enforced by hidden cameras. Tourists can receive fines by mail after returning home.

items.label: Parking & tolls
items.text: Parking in Athens city center is metered and expensive (€1-2/hour); use parking apps like ParkWhiz. Highway tolls apply on national roads and can be paid with cash or by tag.
Rental cars are frequently targeted by thieves, especially in Athens; never leave valuables visible and use guarded parking. Car break-ins are common at popular tourist spots.

items.label: Athens International Airport
items.text: Eleftherios Venizelos (ATH) is Greece's largest hub, located 33 km east of the city. The metro Line 3 connects directly to central Athens in 40 minutes for €10; buses and taxis also serve the airport.

items.label: Regional airports
items.text: Thessaloniki (SKG), Crete (HER), Rhodes (RHO), and other islands have international terminals with direct EU flights. Budget airlines like Ryanair and Wizz Air operate many routes with tight connections.
Airport taxis outside official stands are unlicensed; use the official white taxi rank or call ahead for a fixed-price transfer (€35-45 to central Athens).

items.label: Baggage & connectivity
items.text: WiFi is free in terminals but slow; charge devices in the airport lounge or buy a local SIM for data before departure. Baggage storage is available but costs €5-8 per item.
Summer delays are common due to high traffic; arrive 3 hours early for international flights and expect queues at security and boarding in July-August.

items.label: Ferry operators
items.text: Hellenic Seaways, Anek Lines, and Blue Star Ferries are the major operators; book via GTP.gr, Ferryhopper.com, or directly on operator websites. Advance booking saves money and guarantees seating.

items.label: Popular routes
items.text: Athens (Piraeus) to Mykonos, Crete, and Santorini are daily services; Thessaloniki serves the Sporades islands. Journey times range from 2 hours (speedboats) to 12+ hours (slow ferries to Crete).

items.label: Ferry classes
items.text: Standard seating is cheapest; cabins cost €30-80 extra but are worth it on overnight journeys. Speedboats cost 2-3× more but cut travel time in half.
Summer seas can be rough; if prone to seasickness, take medication before boarding and sit on the lower deck. Ferries occasionally cancel due to weather with little notice.

items.label: Port arrival
items.text: Piraeus (Athens main port) is chaotic; arrive early and follow signs to the correct terminal. Local buses connect Piraeus to central Athens or use a taxi/Beat.

items.label: Unified ticketing
items.text: Athens transport (metro, bus, tram) uses single integrated tickets; buy from kiosks, machines, or the Ticketland app. Tickets are cheaper bought in advance than on-board.

items.label: Tourist passes value
items.text: The 3-day pass (€22) pays for itself after 4-5 journeys; if staying longer, the weekly pass (€30) covers unlimited travel including airport metro. Non-tourists can buy monthly passes (€50) via the Ticketland app.
Ticket inspectors conduct random checks on buses and metro; fines for traveling without a valid ticket start at €50 and increase to €200 if inspectors suspect deliberate evasion.

items.label: Ferry & train booking apps
items.text: Use GTP.gr, Ferryhopper, or Rome2Rio to compare all transport options and book directly; these apps show prices across operators in one place.

items.label: Validation & validity
items.text: Validate paper tickets by scanning them on machines at entry (metro, tram) or on-board (buses); single tickets expire 90 minutes after validation, while passes are calendar-based.

items.label: Local providers
items.text: Vodafone, Cosmote, and Wind are Greece's main carriers; all offer prepaid SIM cards at airport kiosks, supermarkets, and convenience stores for €5-10 with starter credit.

items.label: Data plans
items.text: A 5GB prepaid plan costs €10-15 for 30 days; these are cheaper and faster than airport WiFi or roaming. Activate in-store or online; you'll receive an activation code via SMS.

items.label: WiFi availability
items.text: Most hotels, restaurants, and cafes offer free WiFi, though speeds vary. Download offline maps (Google Maps, Maps.me) before traveling outside cities as connectivity drops in remote areas.
International roaming is expensive; EU roaming rules apply for EU citizens but non-EU visitors face high per-GB charges. Always get a local SIM or enable airplane mode to avoid surprise bills.

items.label: eSIM option
items.text: Major providers Vodafone and Cosmote offer eSIM activation online; purchase before arrival if your phone supports eSIM to avoid airport queues.
